findings.summary: Single Settings class owns configuration for both OCR and classifier domains
evidence: Settings.__init__ loads OCR-specific settings (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS) and classifier-specific settings (CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAG_LIMIT, etc.) in the same class
Both ocr.daemon and classifier.daemon import and instantiate the full Settings object, even though each only uses a subset of its attributes
CLASSIFY_* prefixed fields (lines 32-34, 53-61) and OCR_* prefixed fields (lines 24-26, 30, 43-44) coexist, indicating two separate concerns merged into one class
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting Settings into a base CommonSettings plus OcrSettings and ClassifierSettings subclasses, or using composition with domain-specific config dataclasses. This would make each daemon's configuration surface explicit and prevent accidental cross-domain coupling.

findings.summary: httpx is used both directly and indirectly via openai SDK, with explicit trust_env workaround
evidence: httpx is declared as a direct dependency in pyproject.toml but is also pulled in transitively by the openai SDK
library_setup.py creates an httpx.Client(trust_env=False) specifically to pass to the OpenAI SDK (line 24-25), working around the SDK's internal httpx usage
paperless.py and daemon_loop.py also use httpx directly for Paperless API calls and exception handling
findings.suggestion: This is a minor observation -- httpx serves both as the HTTP client for Paperless API and as the transport layer for openai. The direct dependency declaration is correct since paperless.py uses it independently. No action required, but documenting the dual role in pyproject.toml comments would aid future maintainers.

findings.summary: Full Settings object passed through many layers rather than consumed at boundaries
evidence: ClassificationProcessor.__init__ takes doc, paperless_client, classifier, taxonomy_cache, AND settings as 5 separate params
Worker then reads individual settings fields in multiple methods (_truncate_content, _requeue_for_ocr, _apply_classification)
OcrProcessor similarly takes 4 params including settings, then reads individual fields throughout
findings.suggestion: The worker constructors could accept a narrower config or derive needed values at construction time rather than holding the full Settings bag and reaching into it in every method

findings.summary: Each document creates a fresh PaperlessClient and provider, duplicating HTTP connection setup
evidence: classifier/daemon.py _process_document: creates PaperlessClient(settings) and ClassificationProvider(settings) per document
ocr/daemon.py _process_document: creates PaperlessClient(settings) and OcrProvider(settings) per document
PaperlessClient creates a new httpx.Client with auth headers each time
findings.suggestion: Consider using a per-thread client via ThreadLocal or passing a factory that reuses connections within a thread, rather than creating and tearing down HTTP clients per document

findings.summary: OcrProcessor.process() is 85 lines with nested try/finally blocks mixing lock lifecycle and processing logic
evidence: process() spans lines 50-135 with a try/finally wrapping another try/finally
Multiple concerns in one method: document refresh, error-tag check, claim, download, convert, OCR, assembly, update, release, stats logging, elapsed-time tracking
findings.suggestion: Extract the inner block (download through update) into a _run_ocr_pipeline method to flatten nesting and separate lock lifecycle from processing logic

findings.summary: ClassificationProvider._COMPAT_PARAMS uses string-based method dispatch via getattr
evidence: _COMPAT_PARAMS stores method names as strings: ('temperature', '_is_temperature_error', 'temperature_retries')
_try_strip_compat_param calls getattr(self, detector_name)(error) to dispatch
findings.suggestion: Store direct method references or use a small match/if chain instead of string-based getattr dispatch to improve readability and IDE navigability

findings.summary: Settings class holds 30+ attributes for both OCR and classification concerns in a single flat class
evidence: Settings.__init__ calls 7 private _load_* methods covering API, LLM, tags, daemon, image, logging, and classification settings
OCR-specific fields (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS) sit alongside classification-specific fields (CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_TAXONOMY_LIMIT, etc.)
Both daemons receive the full Settings object even though each uses only a subset of fields
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting into a base SettingsBase with shared fields and OCRSettings/ClassifySettings subclasses, or at minimum grouping related fields into nested dataclasses (e.g. settings.ocr.dpi, settings.classify.max_chars). This would make each daemon's actual dependencies explicit.

findings.summary: _apply_classification rechecks OCR error conditions after the LLM has already been called, mixing validation with application logic
evidence: process() validates content is non-empty at line 106 before calling the LLM
_apply_classification (line 252) then re-checks needs_error_tag(content) at line 261, which detects refusal markers in the same content that was already used for LLM classification
This means the LLM call was wasted if the content had refusal markers -- the check should happen before invoking the classifier
findings.suggestion: Move the needs_error_tag(content) check into the main process() method, after content validation but before calling classify_text(), to avoid wasting an LLM call on content that will be rejected.

findings.summary: OpenAIChatMixin uses inheritance (mixin) where composition with a shared client wrapper would be more direct
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin provides _create_completion (retried API call), _init_stats, reset_stats, and get_stats
Both OcrProvider and ClassificationProvider inherit from it but use it purely for the shared _create_completion call and stats bookkeeping

The mixin accesses self.settings implicitly, creating a hidden coupling contract documented only in the docstring ('The mixin expects self.settings to expose MAX_RETRIES and MAX_RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS')
findings.suggestion: A composed LLMClient class (holding the retry-wrapped completion call and concurrency limiter) passed into each provider's __init__ would make the dependency explicit and eliminate the mixin's implicit self.settings requirement. The stats helpers could remain as the standalone ThreadSafeStats which already exists.

findings.summary: common/__init__.py has no re-exports while classifier/ and ocr/ __init__.py files define __all__ with curated public APIs
evidence: classifier/__init__.py re-exports ClassificationProcessor, ClassificationProvider, ClassificationResult, TaxonomyCache, parse_classification_response
ocr/__init__.py re-exports OcrProcessor, OcrProvider, OCR_ERROR_MARKER, assemble_full_text, bytes_to_images
common/__init__.py contains only a docstring with no re-exports and no __all__
Callers of common always use deep imports (from common.config import Settings, from common.paperless import PaperlessClient) which is fine, but the pattern inconsistency is notable
findings.suggestion: This is a minor inconsistency. Since common is a utility/infrastructure package with many independent modules, the deep-import pattern is arguably more appropriate. Either add a brief comment in common/__init__.py explaining this design choice, or leave as-is since the current pattern works well for a utility package.

findings.summary: Daemon loop catches a wide tuple of exception types including TypeError/KeyError, swallowing them with only a log message
evidence: Line 106 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) in the polling loop. TypeError and KeyError typically indicate programming bugs, not transient failures, yet they are treated identically to network errors and silently retried.
findings.suggestion: Narrow the catch clause to transient exceptions (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError). Let TypeError and KeyError propagate so bugs are surfaced rather than silently retried in a loop.

findings.summary: Several tag-lifecycle helpers log exceptions but do not propagate or signal failure to callers
evidence: remove_stale_queue_tag (line 65-72), release_processing_tag (lines 93-113) catch PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS and only log. The callers have no way to know the operation failed. While this is intentional best-effort, the pattern is repeated verbatim across multiple functions.
findings.suggestion: Return a bool success indicator from these helpers so callers can optionally act on failure, or at minimum centralize the catch-and-log-only pattern into a single wrapper to reduce repetition.

findings.summary: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS bundles ValueError and KeyError with network errors, broadening catch clauses beyond I/O boundaries
evidence: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS = (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError) at line 23. Every caller that catches this tuple will also suppress programming errors like missing dict keys or bad type conversions, not just network/HTTP failures.
findings.suggestion: Consider splitting into separate tuples: one for network/HTTP errors and one for response-parsing errors. Callers that need both can union them explicitly, making intent clearer.

findings.summary: E2E tests use stateful mocks rather than exercising real HTTP or subprocess boundaries
evidence: test_ocr_workflow.py builds a _make_stateful_client mock with side_effect functions to simulate tag state changes. While more realistic than unit mocks, these tests never touch a real HTTP layer or subprocess, making them closer to integration tests than true e2e tests.
findings.suggestion: Consider renaming the e2e directory to integration_stateful or adding a note that true e2e tests require a running Paperless instance. This prevents false confidence about end-to-end coverage.

findings.summary: bootstrap_daemon orchestration has no dedicated tests despite being a critical startup path
evidence: test_bootstrap.py exists but bootstrap_daemon coordinates Settings creation, logging, library setup, signal handlers, preflight checks, and stale lock recovery. A failure in this sequence leaves the daemon silently dead (returns None). The coordination logic merits direct testing.
findings.suggestion: Add tests that verify bootstrap_daemon returns None on ValueError from Settings, on PreflightError from preflight checks, and returns (settings, client) on success.

findings.summary: Several trivial 1-3 line functions carry multi-line docstrings that restate the code
evidence: request_shutdown() is a one-liner (_shutdown_event.set()) yet the module-level docstring describes it. is_openai_client_ready() (return self._client is not None) has clear naming that makes the docstring 'Return whether the client is ready' redundant. LLMConcurrencyLimiter.acquire() docstring 'Acquire a slot; no-op when unlimited' restates the 4-line body.
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on trivial functions where the name and signature already convey full intent. Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behavior or contract details.

findings.summary: Tests uniformly carry # Arrange / # Act / # Assert comments even on trivial 3-line tests
evidence: test_returns_true_immediately_when_tag_id_is_none in test_claims.py has 3 meaningful lines but carries Arrange/Act/Assert markers. This pattern is applied consistently across all test files regardless of test complexity, which is a hallmark of LLM-generated test code.
findings.suggestion: Reserve section comments for complex tests with multi-step setup. For simple tests, the code is self-documenting without ceremony.

findings.summary: common/ package has 16 files which is borderline for a flat package
evidence: common/ contains 16 files mixing config, client, tags, claims, retry, concurrency, etc.
findings.suggestion: Consider grouping related files into subpackages if the package continues to grow

findings.summary: Single Settings class serves both OCR and classifier daemons
evidence: Settings class has OCR-specific fields (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE) alongside classifier-specific fields (CLASSIFY_PRE_TAG_ID)
findings.suggestion: Acceptable at current scale but consider splitting if a third daemon is added
